title: Micah 6
subtitle: What Does the LORD Require? Act Justly, Love Mercy

sections.header: Verses 1-5 — "My People, What Have I Done to You?"

heb.paragraph: The chapter opens with a covenant lawsuit (rib) in which YHWH summons the mountains as witnesses and brings charges against his people. Unlike the rib of chapters 1-3, where the charge was apostasy, here the form is reversed: God is the one who speaks in self-defense, asking 'What have I done to you? How have I burdened you?' The divine plaintiff becomes the divine defendant, creating one of the most emotionally powerful passages in the prophetic corpus.

panels.ctx: The covenant lawsuit of chapter 6 is unique in its rhetorical structure: instead of prosecuting Israel, YHWH invites Israel to prosecute him. The mountains and foundations of the earth are summoned as witnesses (v. 2), echoing the Deuteronomic tradition of cosmic witnesses to the covenant (Deut 4:26; 30:19; 32:1). God's defense consists of a historical recital of saving acts: the exodus, the provision of Moses, Aaron, and Miriam, and the Balaam episode (Num 22-24). The question 'What have I done to you?' (v. 3) is not rhetorical but genuinely plaintive: God asks what he has done to deserve Israel's abandonment. The phrase 'How have I burdened you?' (mah helʾetikha) uses the same verb for 'weary' that Isaiah uses: 'You have burdened me with your sins' (Isa 43:24). The inversion is poignant: Israel accuses God of being burdensome, while God has borne Israel's burden since the exodus.

cross.note: The Song of Moses, which similarly summons cosmic witnesses and recites YHWH's saving acts.
The Balaam episode that God recalls: Balak's plot to curse Israel was transformed by God into blessing.
Isaiah's inversion: God is burdened by Israel's sins, not the reverse.

notes.note: The summons to mountains as witnesses draws on the ancient Near Eastern treaty tradition where natural features served as witnesses to covenant agreements. The 'foundations of the earth' adds cosmic permanence to the proceedings.

God's self-defense is devastating in its tenderness: 'What have I done to you?' is a question a wounded lover asks. The historical recital (exodus, Moses, Aaron, Miriam) demonstrates that God's entire history with Israel has been one of deliverance, not burden.

The Balaam reference is strategically placed: even when enemies sought to destroy Israel through curses, God intervened to transform curses into blessings (Num 23:11-12). The phrase 'from Shittim to Gilgal' encompasses the Jordan crossing — the entry into the promised land.

Calvin was deeply moved by the divine question: God condescends to ask what offense he has committed against his people. This is not the language of an offended sovereign but of a hurt parent or spouse.

The verb hel'etikha ('I have burdened/wearied you') uses the Hiphil of la'ah ('to be weary'). The question implies that Israel has grown tired of God's demands, to which God responds: what demands? I have only delivered you.

Stuart identifies this as a 'reverse rib': instead of God prosecuting Israel, God invites Israel to prosecute him. The rhetorical effect is profound: Israel cannot find a single charge to bring against the God who has done nothing but save them.

Waltke considers this passage the emotional heart of the book. The God who thundered in theophany (1:2-4) and pronounced Zion's destruction (3:12) now speaks with the vulnerability of a wounded relationship. The shift from cosmic Judge to plaintive lover is Micah's most brilliant rhetorical move.

sections.header: Verses 6-8 — "Act Justly, Love Mercy, Walk Humbly"

heb.paragraph: In v. 8, hesed appears as the second of three divine requirements: 'love mercy.' The verb ahavat ('to love') governs hesed, indicating that YHWH demands not merely the practice of mercy but the love of it — an interior disposition, not mere external compliance. This is the ethical counterpart to Hosea 6:6 ('I desire mercy, not sacrifice').

The infinitive absolute of tsanaʿ ('to be modest, humble') modifies 'walking with God.' The word occurs only here in the OT, making it a hapax legomenon. Its rarity underscores its importance: humble walking is not a generic virtue but a specific, distinctive quality of the covenant relationship. Some translate it 'attentively' or 'circumspectly,' suggesting careful attention to God's presence in every step.

panels.ctx: Verse 8 is arguably the most famous verse in the minor prophets and one of the most important ethical summaries in the entire Bible. The rhetorical build-up (vv. 6-7) escalates through increasingly extravagant offerings: burnt offerings, calves a year old, thousands of rams, ten thousand rivers of oil, even the sacrifice of a firstborn child. Each offer is more extreme than the last, reaching the point of human sacrifice to demonstrate the futility of the sacrificial approach. The answer (v. 8) is startling in its simplicity: what God requires is not ritual performance at any scale but three relational qualities: justice (mishpat), mercy (hesed), and humble walking (hatsneaʿ lekhet). The triad corresponds to the three dimensions of covenant life: social (justice toward others), relational (loyalty toward God and neighbor), and personal (humility before God).

cross.note: Hosea's parallel: 'I desire mercy, not sacrifice, and acknowledgment of God rather than burnt offerings.'
Isaiah's parallel: 'I have more than enough of burnt offerings... Stop doing wrong. Learn to do right! Seek justice.'
Jesus cites 'justice, mercy, and faithfulness' as the 'more important matters of the law,' a clear echo of Micah 6:8.
James defines 'pure religion' in terms that echo Micah: caring for orphans and widows and keeping oneself unstained.

notes.note: The escalating offers parody the sacrificial mentality: if one calf isn't enough, try thousands of rams; if rams aren't enough, try rivers of oil; if oil isn't enough, try your firstborn child. The absurdity exposes the fundamental error: God cannot be bought.

This verse distills the entire ethical tradition of the OT into three requirements. Justice (mishpat) addresses social relationships; mercy (hesed) addresses covenantal loyalty; humble walking addresses the posture of the human heart before God. Together they constitute a complete ethical program.

Calvin noted that the escalation to child sacrifice demonstrates how far human religion can deviate when it seeks to earn divine favor through performance. The greater the sacrifice, the greater the self-deception.

Calvin considered this verse the clearest summary of the moral law in the entire OT. It teaches that God's requirements are accessible to everyone: they do not demand extraordinary resources but ordinary faithfulness.

The three infinitive constructs (asot mishpat, ahavat hesed, hatsnea lekhet) form a tricolon of ethical requirements. The first and third are actions (doing justice, walking humbly); the second is an attitude (loving mercy). The structure suggests that justice and humility are the external expressions of an internal disposition of covenant love.

Stuart reads the escalating sacrificial offers as a parody of the Canaanite-influenced worship that characterized the northern and, increasingly, the southern cult. The inclusion of child sacrifice (v. 7) is not hypothetical but reflects the actual practices of Ahaz's reign (2 Kgs 16:3). Micah's response (v. 8) is a prophetic Torah: the law reduced to its essential core.

Waltke argues that this verse functions as the ethical summary not only of Micah but of the entire prophetic movement. The three requirements correspond to three theological traditions: justice (the Mosaic legal tradition), mercy (the covenantal tradition of Hosea), and humble walking (the wisdom tradition). Micah synthesizes all three into a single, comprehensive ethic.

heb.paragraph: The 'short ephah' (v. 10) is a fraudulent dry measure used to cheat buyers. An ephah was the standard unit for grain (approximately 22 liters); a 'short' ephah gave less than the standard amount. This economic fraud is directly connected to the theological indictment: the same people who worship with elaborate sacrifices cheat their neighbors in the marketplace.

panels.ctx: After the ethical summary (v. 8), the chapter returns to indictment. The city is addressed directly (v. 9), and the charges are specific: dishonest scales, short measures, violence by the wealthy, lies, and deceit. The reference to 'the statutes of Omri and all the practices of Ahab's house' (v. 16) is remarkable: Micah traces Judah's corruption to the influence of the northern dynasty most associated with Baal worship. The futility curses (vv. 14-15) follow the Deuteronomic pattern: eating without satisfaction, storing without saving, planting without harvesting, pressing olives without using oil, crushing grapes without drinking wine. Every effort produces nothing. The city that practiced fraud will experience cosmic futility.

cross.note: The covenant curses of futile labor that Micah applies to Jerusalem's commercial fraud.
Amos's parallel indictment of dishonest merchants who use rigged scales and short measures.
The reigns of Omri and Ahab that established the Baal cult in the northern kingdom.

notes.note: The city's economic corruption is catalogued with legal precision: dishonest scales, short measures, violence, lies. These are covenant violations with specific Mosaic penalties (Lev 19:35-36; Deut 25:13-16).

The futility curses are the Deuteronomic covenant penalties in action: every investment fails, every harvest evaporates, every store empties. God turns the economy itself into an instrument of judgment.
The reference to Omri and Ahab traces Judah's corruption to its northern source. The southern kingdom has adopted the worst practices of the dynasty that introduced systematic Baal worship to Israel.
Calvin applied the dishonest-scales indictment to every form of commercial fraud: the principle extends beyond weights and measures to every transaction where one party deceives another.

The phrase huqqot 'omri ('statutes of Omri') is unique in the OT. The NET note observes that Omri is not mentioned elsewhere in the prophets; his appearance here suggests that Micah traces the corruption to the founding of Samaria itself (1 Kgs 16:24) and the dynasty that commercialized Baal worship.

Stuart reads the indictment as targeting Jerusalem's merchant class, who have adopted the commercial practices of Omri's dynasty. The 'statutes of Omri' may refer to commercial regulations favorable to foreign trade that came at the expense of covenant justice.

Waltke sees the commercial fraud indictment as the practical antithesis of 6:8. Those who should 'act justly' instead use dishonest scales; those who should 'love mercy' instead practice violence; those who should 'walk humbly' instead follow the arrogant statutes of Omri. The indictment mirrors the ethical summary point by point.

themes.note: Justice and covenant tie at maximum: the chapter moves from covenant lawsuit to ethical summary to social justice indictment. Mercy scores very high because hesed is one of the three requirements of 6:8.

rows.label: vv.6-7
rows.text: Escalating sacrifices: from calves to firstborn

rows.label: v.8
rows.text: THE requirement: justice, mercy, humble walking

rows.label: vv.13-16
rows.text: Futility curses and statutes of Omri

lit.note: The chapter moves from covenant lawsuit (vv. 1-5) through the ethical summit (vv. 6-8) to renewed indictment (vv. 9-16). Verse 8 is the theological center of the entire book and one of the most important ethical statements in the Bible.

hebtext.note: The chapter opens with a covenant lawsuit (rib) in which YHWH summons the mountains as witnesses and brings charges against his people. Unlike the rib of chapters 1-3, where the charge was apostasy, here the form is reversed: God is the one who speaks in self-defense, asking 'What have I done to you? How have I burdened you?' The divine plaintiff becomes the divine defendant, creating one of the most emotionally powerful passages in the prophetic corpus.

In v. 8, hesed appears as the second of three divine requirements: 'love mercy.' The verb ahavat ('to love') governs hesed, indicating that YHWH demands not merely the practice of mercy but the love of it — an interior disposition, not mere external compliance. This is the ethical counterpart to Hosea 6:6 ('I desire mercy, not sacrifice').

The infinitive absolute of tsanaʿ ('to be modest, humble') modifies 'walking with God.' The word occurs only here in the OT, making it a hapax legomenon. Its rarity underscores its importance: humble walking is not a generic virtue but a specific, distinctive quality of the covenant relationship. Some translate it 'attentively' or 'circumspectly,' suggesting careful attention to God's presence in every step.

The 'short ephah' (v. 10) is a fraudulent dry measure used to cheat buyers. An ephah was the standard unit for grain (approximately 22 liters); a 'short' ephah gave less than the standard amount. This economic fraud is directly connected to the theological indictment: the same people who worship with elaborate sacrifices cheat their neighbors in the marketplace.

ppl.name: Micah
ppl.text: 16) is remarkable: Micah traces Judah's corruption to the influence of the northern dynasty most associated with Baal worship…

ppl.name: Balaam
ppl.role: Mesopotamian seer hired to curse Israel
ppl.text: Balaam son of Beor is attested outside the Bible — the Deir Alla inscription (c.800 BC) names him as a seer from the region. In Num 22–24 he is hired by Balak to curse Israel but God compels him to bless instead, producing four increasingly exalted oracles including the messianic "star from Jacob" (Num 24:17). His donkey sees what he cannot; his death in Num 31:8 confirms his role in the Baal Peor seduction (Num 31:16; Rev 2:14).

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Aaron
ppl.text: Aaron, Moses’s brother, is consecrated as Israel’s first high priest in Lev 8–9. He bears the names of the twelve tribes on his breastplate before God and enters the Most Holy Place once a year on Yom Kippur (Lev 16). His failures — the golden calf (Exod 32), challenging Moses’s authority (Num 12), complicity at Meribah (Num 20) — make him the paradigm of the imperfect priest who prefigures a better high priest (Heb 7:26–28).

ppl.name: Miriam
ppl.role: Prophetess and worship leader; Moses’s sister
ppl.text: Miriam leads Israel’s women in worship after the Red Sea crossing (Exod 15:20) and is called a prophetess. In Num 12 she challenges Moses’s unique prophetic authority alongside Aaron and is struck with ṣāraʿat — only to be healed after Moses’s seven-word intercession. She dies at Kadesh (Num 20:1), her passing noted in two terse Hebrew words before the water crisis.

trans.title: Comparing translations in What Does the LORD Require? Act Justly, Love Mercy

translations.text: I brought you up out of Egypt and redeemed you from the land of slavery. I sent Moses to lead you, also Aaron and Miriam.
For I brought you up from the land of Egypt and redeemed you from the house of slavery, and I sent before you Moses, Aaron, and Miriam.
Listen to what the Lord says: “Stand up, plead my case before the mountains; let the hills hear what you have to say.
Hear what the LORD says: Arise, plead your case before the mountains, and let the hills hear your voice.

debate: The verb hel'etikha ('I have burdened/wearied you') uses the Hiphil of la'ah ('to be weary'). The question implies that Israel has grown tired of God'…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads What Does the LORD Require? Act Justly, Love Mercy within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
